text:
Experiment 1: Seeing Slices with Colored Water

In this experiment, we will predict the polygon shape that results by tipping a container containing some colored water.

Make a Prediction

  • If we slice a rectangular prism or a right rectangular pyramid, what polygon shapes will appear at the cross section of the slice?

Conduct the Experiment

Equipment

  • Clear plastic cube or rectangular prism
  • Clear plastic pyramid
  • Colored water
  • Rubber bands

Directions (individually or with a partner or small group)

  • Pour some colored water into the three-dimensional shape.
  • Tilt the three-dimensional shape to imagine slicing through it.
  • Use rubber bands to outline the cross section of the slice.
  • Make a sketch of the polygon created by the slice.

Analyze the Data

  • If you slice a cube or rectangular prism, what are the polygon shapes formed by the cross section?
  • If you slice a pyramid, what are the polygon shapes formed by the cross section?

Experiment 2: Slicing a Prism Made from Clay

In this experiment, we will predict the polygon shape that results by slicing a cube of modeling clay.

Make a Prediction

  • If we slice a rectangular prism, what polygon shapes will appear at the cross section of the slice?

Conduct an Experiment

Equipment:

  • Modeling clay
  • Clay wire cutter or plastic knife

Directions:(individually or with a partner or small group)

  • Shape the clay into a square prism.
  • Make one or more slices of the square prism.
  • Describe the shape that results.
  • Make a sketch of the cutes needed to make the shape.
  • Make a sketch of the polygon created by the slice

Analyze the Data

  • Describe the minimum number of cuts needed to make each shape.

Answers will vary. Possible answers include:

A triangle prism (view from the top of the prism)

  • One vertical cut that passes through opposite vertices of the square top will form two congruent triangular prisms. The red line in the diagram shows cutting from the square top.

tiles.title: Top view of cutting a triangle prism

text:
A pentagonal prism (view from the top of the prism)

  • One vertical cut that takes off a “corner” of the square top will produce a pentagonal prism (but not a regular pentagonal prism). The red line in the diagram shows cutting from the square top.

tiles.title: Top view of cutting a pentagonal prism

text:
A hexagonal prism (view from the top of the prism)

  • Two vertical cuts that take off “corners” of the square top will produce a hexagonal prism (but not a regular hexagonal prism). The red lines in the diagram shows cutting from the square top.

tiles.title: Top view of cutting hexagonal prisms
